name: Turkish Isparta Highlands

staple_foods.name: Golden Delicious apple

primary_metabolites.fructose_concentration: 5.5-7.2 g/100g [Isparta highland apple — Özturk et al. 2015 Turkish J Agric For 39(4)]
primary_metabolites.ascorbic_acid: 0.18-0.28 g/100g [Isparta highland apple 1000-1200m — Özturk et al. 2015; high UV at Taurus foothills]

organic_acids.malic_acid: 0.55-0.70 g/100g [Golden Delicious, Isparta highlands 1000-1200m — Özturk et al. 2015 Turkish J Agric For; high-altitude diurnal cooling retains malic acid]

staple_foods.notes: Golden Delicious apple grown in Isparta Province (1000-1200m), Turkey's most important apple-producing region. Taurus Mountain limestone parent material, semi-continental climate with cold winters (high temperature seasonality). Malic acid 0.55-0.70 g/100g — highest in the apple natural experiment set along with RSU-14 Shanxi highlands (0.48-0.53). The higher value at equivalent altitude (1100m vs 1050m) likely reflects the colder winter temperatures of continental Isparta vs monsoonal Shanxi, consistent with the temperature-malic hypothesis. Ascorbic acid elevated (0.18-0.28) due to high UV at Anatolian plateau altitude. Note: limestone soil (pH 7.5) vs acidic volcanic soil (RSU-13/14) — soil chemistry may modulate malic acid transport in addition to temperature.
data_sources: Özturk et al. (2015) — Physicochemical and nutritional properties of apple cultivars grown in Isparta, Turkish J Agric For 39(4):549
Zhao et al. (2021) — Effects of genetic background and altitude on apple metabolites, Foods 10(12):2950

staple_foods.name: wheat bread (Türk ekmeği)
staple_foods.notes: Ekmek (wheat bread) is the dominant carb — standard Turkish white bread from regional wheat.

staple_foods.name: lamb (Anatolian highland)
staple_foods.notes: Anatolian highland lamb from semi-extensive grazing on Taurus mountain pastures. Akkaraman and Morkaraman breeds common. Isparta is a major sheep-producing province.
data_sources: Maga (1983) — Flavor potentiators, CRC Crit Rev Food Sci Nutr 18:231

staple_foods.name: rose petal (Isparta damask rose, Rosa damascena)

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 200-500 mg GAE/100g fresh petals [Rosa damascena — Baydar et al. 2004 Ind Crops Prod 19:103]

terpenes.linalool: 30-50% of rose essential oil [Rosa damascena Isparta — Baydar et al. 2004; geraniol and citronellol co-dominant]

staple_foods.notes: Isparta province produces ~65% of the world's rose oil (attar of roses) from Rosa damascena. Rose petals are harvested at dawn before linalool volatilizes. The combination of Isparta's altitude (1000-1100m), cool spring temperatures, and limestone soils is credited with producing the highest-quality rose oil globally — a terroir claim supported by comparative essential oil analyses (Baydar et al. 2004). This is an outstanding example of agricultural terroir beyond food crops. The rose-linalool content is one of the highest recorded in any non-aromatic-category crop in this dataset.
data_sources: Baydar et al. (2004) — Oil content, essential oil composition and fatty acid content of Rosa damascena, Ind Crops Prod 19:103

staple_foods.name: yogurt (süt yoğurt, Isparta highland)

organic_acids.lactic_acid: 0.6-1.4 g/100g [traditional Turkish yogurt — Tamime & Robinson 2007 Yoghurt: Science and Technology]

umami_compounds.glutamate: ~0.5-1.5 g/100g free glutamate [fermented dairy — proteolysis during fermentation; Ninomiya 1998]

staple_foods.notes: Strained and unstrained yogurt is central to Turkish cuisine — used as sauce, condiment, and standalone food. Isparta highland milk from Akkaraman sheep and cattle. Turkish yogurt fermentation uses Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus + Streptococcus thermophilus.
data_sources: Tamime & Robinson (2007) — Yoghurt: Science and Technology, 3rd ed. Woodhead Publishing

notes: Isparta Province, Taurus Mountain foothills (~1100m). Limestone sedimentary soils (pH 7.5 — notable contrast to acidic volcanic soils of Shanxi), semi-continental climate with cold winters. Key natural experiment RSU: Golden Delicious apple at 1100m — highest-altitude in the 4-RSU apple altitude gradient (RSU-13 at 600m, RSU-49 at 800m, RSU-14 at 1050m, RSU-50 at 1100m). Expected malic acid 0.55-0.70 g/100g — highest in the apple set, anchoring the high end of the altitude-malic regression. Rose petal (Rosa damascena) is a unique aromatic with well-studied terroir claims.
